text:
*** The Shoal lined up behind Melusine and together they descended into the temple beneath the city. The black basalt steps had been worn slick by thousands of feet, if not millions. From the youngest to the eldest, every merfolk of Geynea went down every third new moon to fulfill their sacred duty—the purpose of their existence—and feed the last surviving shard of the Sky Gods.

They wound down seven towers of spiral stairs, then through seven corridors. The slow press at least gave everyone time to admire the carvings cut into the volcanic rock that lined the passages. Everyone knew the story; like this, they rehearsed its stations again and again. The tale of how their kind had been made, so they would not forget that they were guardians of the world’s peace, serving the Light from the deep, hidden from the sun.

Melusine didn’t dare run her hand across the carvings. They were already worn smooth, and lately she had needed Tydea’s courage—the Last Sky God, Lady of the Moon—more than ever, to draw strength from her memory. But the closer the stone-cut story came to its end, to Tydea’s sacrifice, the more a pressure grew at the back of Melusine’s mind. It always began as a dull headache. Then as if something slick, a tentacle, a parasite testing the bone for a way in, moved behind her skull. The nearer they came to the temple’s heart, the stronger it pressed.

With the pressure came whispers. Nonsense sounds like wind in a chasm. Then alien thoughts. Bloodlust. A hunger to tear out her sisters’ throats, then rip the temple down stone by stone. An urge to cut her own throat when it was done—or else to kneel to the Gods of the Depths and rise with them for the surface, to destroy everything.

The Gods of the Depths were angry at their confinement. Their ever-seething malice found cracks in the prison when Tydea’s power over them had weakened. Every merfolk was told this a hundred times, a thousand: these were not their own thoughts. Resist with all your strength. And if you could not, begin to sing the Song, softly. It was the only thing that could check the Gods of the Depths, even a little.

By the time they reached the center, the Song was audible from every side, hummed by so many for their own calm and sanity. Melusine could not imagine meeting that ancient force out in the ocean, in her water-form. How fast her mind would have broken. In their human forms they carried the memory of the Light more strongly, and were somewhat better shielded against such attacks. That was why no merfolk dared be in the water at new moon. Those who had ever been caught outside became slaves to the Gods of the Depths forever.

At the end of the corridor they stepped into a vast circular hall, the heart of the temple. Along the wall a tight spiral of stone steps continued downward, and the city’s entire population lined it. To the north stood the priestesses’ caste in stark white robes; beside them, officials of the middle tower, the Queen’s tower, in gold cloaks. East of them gathered the scholars in blue; then the Hunters, breastplated in stiffened sharkskin. Then the gatherers; then those who tended the gardens around Geynea—like the gatherers, they lived most of their lives beyond the city walls, returning only around the new moon to the two southern towers. Opposite the priestesses stood the Tlanik (servant caste), in brown tunics and trousers. On the western side were the craftsmen in ornate red, and finally the masons in purple, charged with holding the city against time’s decay.

Nine castes. Seven towers. All working as one to keep the dark god imprisoned beneath Geynea—Levianoch—sealed away.

Melusine took her place among the Hunters and looked up to the gallery on the north wall, where High Priestess Mareen soon appeared with three attendants. During rites the priestesses wore white bone masks that covered the right half of the face, emblem of the moon. There was no sermon. No prayer. Everyone knew the stories by heart, even the youngest, and there was no one to ask for grace or mercy. The Sky Gods were dead, all of them, and from the Gods of the Depths they could expect nothing.

Mareen began the Song. At the first notes, the Soulstone rose from the floor. It was a great, uncut crystal, and a faint white light pulsed in its heart, weaker and weaker with each cycle. The rest joined the singing, and the priestesses behind Mareen beat drums so no one would slip the rhythm. The Song had no words, only perfect harmonies that vibrated in the air, weaving an unseen net, a shield from which Levianoch’s desperate blows skittered away.

When the Soulstone had fully risen, a corridor that ended at floor level opened and the Guide stepped out. The Guide was always one of their sisters, a volunteer whose soul would lead the victims’ souls into the depths of the Soulstone. Melusine stared, stunned, at the blue-haired woman in white. Her former teacher. Their Shoal’s previous leader, Tyna—the one Mel had replaced at Queen Nerida’s command.

Her throat tightened. Perhaps the former Shoal Leader had seen no other way to serve the memory of the Sky Gods than to offer herself as Guide. It was an honor. Mel could never forget that at this point the woman’s life would end, and no one had a comforting answer for what came after. No one knew what became of the souls in the crystal—whether they lived, or were burned away as energy. For most merfolk even the latter was an uplifting thought. For Melusine, that final extinction filled her with dread. Perhaps that was why she pitied the victims so much.

The hundred humans, drugged to the edge of collapse, shuffled in behind Tyna. The priestesses in white took positions along the steps in a ring and each drew a human man before her, placing him exactly over one of the channels that began at the rim and joined beneath the Soulstone.

The Song shifted harmony. Tyna knelt before the crystal. She drew two deep breaths, and on the third raised the priestesses’ obsidian dagger in both hands and drove it into her belly. She pulled it free so the blood would pour unhindered into the hollow under the stone.

Then the priestess standing at the northernmost point under the gallery raised her dagger and opened the sailor’s throat before her. Then the one to her right. Then the next. One by one the men clutched at their necks, but the Song drowned their death-rattle. When they fell, their blood gathered in the channels and trickled toward the Soulstone in thin streams. With each sacrifice the crystal flared brighter.

When the last of the hundred had bled into the hollow beneath the stone, the Soulstone blazed once more, then, pulsing like the beat of a great heart, sank back into the deep. The Song ended.

The merfolk stood in respectful silence. Then the High Priestess spoke.

“Thank you for your sacrifice, our sister Tyna. Lead the Hundred’s souls, and hope that on the far side you may yet meet the Sky Gods. And let us take the Hundred’s flesh at the sacrificial feast, to honor them as well. Let it feed the bodies of the guardians, so we may continue to defend their world.”
